Hey guys, Nick Nanton, and we are back with some more secrets from the Living Legend formula. We've been talking in the last video all about how there's a thing called the business trifecta, how you've got to have media marketing and PR in your business to make it run on all cylinders. And we've been talking about the secret formula for media success, how you've got to take mass media credentials and insert them in your media. And also, we're going to be talking about some of the secrets that we reveal actually inside the product today. And so I brought a couple friends along, and one of them is right here with us right now, Coach Pete Derruta. Coach, how are you doing today, man? Doing great, Nick. How are you? Doing awesome, man. So one of the things that we talk about inside this product is building your brand and telling your story and finding your niche. Those are some very important things. You've built the brand of Coach Pete off of your story and your niche. Tell us a little bit about the background of that. Well, the background is, Nick, I worked with teachers when I first got out of college. And my last name is Derruta, so it's not the easiest name in the world to remember or pronounce. My dad is a college professor, and his students have always called him Professor D. And so I would go to the schools, and they would make the announcement. I'd be meeting with all the teachers after school. They'd make the announcement over the loudspeaker. And one principal finally said, you know, your name is so hard to pronounce. I'm going to start calling you Coach Pete because you're the financial coach of the school system here. So I want everyone to know who you are. So Coach Pete stuck from the 1990s all the way up to where we are right now. That's awesome. So one of the things that we talk about in the product is when you tell your story, one of the reasons you tell your story is to create what I call personal hooks. So, you know, that's the abbreviated version of your story, but we already got a couple personal hooks in right there. Your dad was a college professor. You've worked a lot with teachers. It's kind of a cute, funny story about how the coach called you Coach Pete. I can only imagine the students wondering after school what sport you were coaching, right? And so the fact of the matter is that personal hooks are a thing that if you reveal them, they will help more people have something in common with you. Because when you have affinity, like-minded people doing like-minded things and there's affinity, there's similarities there, people actually trust you more. So if my dad was a college professor, me and Coach Pete might have something in common. Also because a college professor is a respected profession, you know that at least Coach Pete's dad was smart. We're not sure about coach, but we know at least his dad was smart, right Coach? That's right, Nick. Awesome. So tell me this, one of the other things we teach is, you know, when you're building your brand, one of the things you've got to have is you've got to have content. And one of the things that I love is you churn out content daily on your radio show. You've got a radio show in about 100 markets around the country and you churn out that content. But let's talk a little bit about what you've done also with like your seven baby steps book to product. Tell us a little bit about some of the secrets you've learned of working with us about taking your content and spreading it even further to build your credibility. Yeah, one of the things about what I've done through my life, I've done so many things and I've worked on so many things and so many topics. I forget more than I know. You hear that saying, but it's true. And what you guys have helped me do is corral a lot of the things that I've been forgetting or not using correctly and packaging them all in a way where it's a direct hit into the prospects or the clients, the current clients into their mind. So I mean, in these days, Nick, we have so many things going on. Matter of fact, we're Skyping right now in Skype. I didn't know what Skype was until about five years ago, but we have cell phones. We have, we have like my daughter's always doing something. So my wife's always having to run around in the evenings, taking her to dance class and gymnastics, that kind of thing. But we get so many things going on that we don't do the things we need to do. Procrastination is alive and well. So the objective to anyone out there who's trying to market is you need to put yourself, you know how we have these football polls and Nick, congratulations, University of Florida, number one this year. But we have the football polls and, you know, they go 25 teams deep, same in basketball. So all we ever look at is the first five or 10. So what you need to do in your marketing day is you need to be in the, in the clients positioning, you need to be in the first five or 10 of things they need to do in the next week. And if you're not, you're down there at number 22. No one can tell me who the number 22 basketball team or football team is, but they can always tell you who number one is. So we try to get up to that number one spot in their mind so that when it comes time to procrastinate, they're going to procrastinate other things to get to you. And you guys have done a great job of helping me package my message again. So it's a direct hit in the client's mind and word number one on their list. If something happens that you start creating a bond or relationship and you start taking over those spots, we call it the Oprah factor. If you watch Oprah every day, Oprah is going to take up one of those 100 relational spots that you can actually have. Now, you're absolutely right. We've got to try to get to that top 10. And you know, blogs and articles and online stuff helps a lot. That's half of it. But the other half of the equation is also physical stuff. One thing that I think is also really important that we talk about is a concept called the ladder of credibility. Now, the ladder of credibility is the fact that, so I'm a lawyer by trade. Let's keep that just between us girls. I don't talk about that much. And you're a financial advisor by trade coach. And the thing about that is that whether you're an advisor or a lawyer or whatever, there's some sort of licensing in our fields. And that's like the baseline. Some people think that that's all that matters. In the days of old, you, like a veterinarian or a dentist or a lawyer, you'd go get your credentials, you'd hang out your shingle, and the business would just fly in. So today, in this day and age, that's the baseline. Everyone as a lawyer has to be licensed. Everyone as a financial advisor is licensed on some level, right? So the fact of the matter is that's just the base. That's the first rung of the ladder, but you cannot stop there. You've got to do other things to help you climb the ladder. And as we've done with you, coach, you become a best-selling author. That's another rung on the ladder. You've been awarded a Quillie Award. That's another rung on the ladder. You've been in USA Today and Wall Street Journal on Newsweek. We did a documentary on you. You've been on the biography channel. You've got the golden reel in your office. So each one of those things is another rung up the ladder. What happens eventually is you climb so high up the ladder that all people can see is you when they look for a solution, and you're just staring down on all your competitors. And so talk a little bit about how each of those methodical things, you have to implement them. They don't just magically fly out from your office and go make magic. You have to implement them. But talk a little bit about how those have affected and helped you, maybe in social media as well as just getting clients and prospects to close deals. Yeah. It's great to get, I've been interviewed by all the major news sources. It's great to be interviewed by them in print especially. But if you don't maximize that, no one's ever going to know. So one of the things is to frame any article you've been in and have it in a real good place in your office, not in the backboard storage room, but out there in the room that people see when they first come in. And being a best-selling author has been great. I mean, I've got books to prove it. But one of the things that's got me more attention than anything is this ring. The best-selling author ring. So I wear this everywhere I go. And I get, I mean, everyone comments on it. They think it's a Super Bowl ring. So it gets into a conversation. And I say it's even more valuable to me than a Super Bowl ring would be. And so it's about making sure that you are tuning your own horn because I learned a long time ago that if you don't promote yourself, no one else is going to do it for you. And with your help, you've helped me get the tools that I need to promote myself. If you can display your credibility, it changes everything. I mean, displaying your credibility, things like, I mean, that the best-sellers really have a great reputation for this ring. That's one of the things when, when I won an Emmy, you know, they didn't give me a lapel pen or anything. And obviously, I would love for people to know that. But again, as you said, coach, you don't want to, you don't want to look arrogant. You don't want to look like you're bragging. You want everything around you to point to your credibility. So when someone comes in your office, you want them to see those articles framed on the wall. You want them to see the golden reel. You want them to see the movie poster framed. You want them to see your ring. You want them to see the Quillie statues. You want everything around you. You want your walls to talk. You want everything to display your expertise for you. And the ring is just a really cool tangible expression of that. Again, when I won the Emmy, I was like, man, I wish they had a ring or something for this. So because we own the National Academy of Best-Selling Authors, I thought, how can we do this? So we went to the people who make the rings for the Super Bowl and for the World Series, and that's literally the same ring. And they make a pretty hefty ring there. And it looks awful good on you, coach. So hey, we're going to get to our final interview here. But thanks a ton for joining us today. And I look forward to continuing to work with you, man. One more thing, Nick. You've done such a great job, and you got me so much good PR and so many great things to show off to clients I've had to buy a new office. All right. Thanks, coach. Have a great day, man. We'll see you soon. Here and now. Hey, guys. Nick Nanton here, and welcome back to this next video installment in the Living Legends series. And this is a real cool video because you've learned some cool concepts by now. You've learned about the business trifecta, how you have to have media, marketing, and PR in your business for it to run on all cylinders. You've learned about the secret formula for media success and how mass media credentials work best when they're inserted in direct media in order to drive marketing and revenue. And so what I thought I'd do on this video is I'd bring in a couple of my friends that I've worked with who understand these concepts and are using them day in and day out. And that's what we're going to do. So I've got my good friend here, Tracy Myers today. Tracy, how are you doing, man? Hey, Nick. Doing good, man. Stuffing with a little laryngeitis, but I'm good. Thanks. That's all right, man. We'll power through, man. Well, I appreciate you being on with me. Let's talk a little bit about what you do. I mean, I know what you do, but tell our viewers what you do. Well, essentially, currently, all in the family business is a car dealership. We've been here, started 85 years ago with my great grandfather who opened the very first Flankmeyer store. And of course, that opportunity, like a lot of other entrepreneurs, they take one opportunity and they are able to jumpstart other opportunities. And for me, that turned into doing everything from consulting other dealerships to training sales consultants and to teaching branding and marketing to car dealerships across the country and in Canada. Awesome. Well, and you're sure you haven't opened a 1,900 hotline the way you're talking today? I have not, but that may be on the agenda next to what I say on you, right? Cool. So let's talk a little bit about, I mean, you do a lot of media. So one of the reasons I want to talk with you is because you're a brick and mortar business selling to consumers. We're also talking to a couple of my clients, one who runs a professional practice and one who is a B2B business in a service business. So I thought, you know, Tracy does a lot of cool stuff in business to consumer and it's actually good. You sell cars, right? I mean, you do a lot of other stuff too, but let's talk a little bit about mass media. We always talk about a television radio newspaper, awesome for two things really, credibility and awareness, unless it's an advertisement. And you do a lot of advertisements. So that's interesting. But then also, you know, we talk about direct media, creating your own media and getting it out in the community. And that can be everything from Facebook and social media to CDs, DVDs, direct mail, newspaper. And the real secret is taking a bunch of the mass media credentials and inserting that stuff in your direct media. Now the good news for me right now is I can see a bunch of your media right behind you. So that doesn't hurt. So good to see. Tell me a little bit about how media and kind of those concepts have worked in your business, because I know you know beyond as much as anyone I know that marketing is the only thing that drives revenue. And that's one of the things I really try to ramp on people. Marketing is the only thing that drives revenue. Your TV commercials are a type of marketing because they're actually direct response, come on into the dealership now stuff. But marketing or media and PR is where people get confused. Let's talk a little bit about how you use all those things together. Sure. Well, it's a great question. I'm glad you asked because it's something that I try to relate to cart dealership owners and general managers and sales managers and marketing experts and gurus within cart dealerships all the time. And I call it layering. And so many people want to actually put out a commercial, whether it's on TV, radio, newspaper, direct mail, and they expect all the results that they're expecting, which is usually more than is actually going to happen. And it just doesn't work that way in most cases anymore. So you have to layer it. And by layering it, of course, you know exactly what this means. You probably call it something different in fact, I know you do. But in layering, for example, when we're filming our TV commercial, we're also shooting a little handheld video from behind the scenes. We're also taking pictures. We're posting that stuff to Instagram. We're posting it to Facebook. We're posting it to Twitter. We're interacting with our friends or our fan base or whatever you want to call them, the people we engage with on social media as well. And we invite them down to participate in the event. We'll put out a press release that we're actually filming the TV commercial the day or a couple of days before. And we actually caught that we're filming a TV show instead of calling it a commercial. So just the different layers that we put together, that's really how we make that pop for us. And I joke all the time that we're the place that puts out a press release that says that we're getting ready to put out a press release. So I think that's how important all those different layers are. I couldn't agree more. And I apologize to you at home. We don't have the best connection, but we can hear good. So that's cool. So, so tell me this. Obviously you built the entire thing that I this is all based off this living legend formula that Dan and I put together is all about building your personal brand and then turning into a legend. And one of the coolest things that you've done is, I mean, you do run crazy commercials in the community, which is awesome. But I even saw an Instagram post you had yesterday that that a guy came into the office who a special needs guy and he just wanted to meet you. Man, talk about that. Well, you know, that was really cool. And the time he couldn't have been more perfect because it obviously wasn't planned. Our interview was planned, but that wasn't planned. This kid had his caretaker, a special needs child, a young adult, rather, and his caregiver, he had been begging his caregiver to bring him by the dealership because he wanted to meet the guy on TV that made him laugh so much. So, and of course, we take pictures of everybody. So, we weren't necessarily taking advantage of that moment because it was a perfect Kodak moment, but we take advantage of all those moments. And because we did, it wasn't awkward to the people who would see that picture and say, oh, they're, you know, they're exploiting this young adult. So, you know, he came in and actually wanted to meet me because to him, I'm a celebrity. And really to everybody in my marketplace, I'm a celebrity because I'm on TV all the time. Not only in the crazy commercials, that's one side of me, but something really, it really is an alter ego, but something that a lot of people don't realize is that I have to take that encounter balance, which we did really well in the Carmen documentary. It showed both sides of me, the alter ego and the TV commercials and who I am as a father, as a person, as somebody that's passionate about what I do and about helping people. And when the local news media, when they need a story or an answer, or they have questions about the automotive industry, like the latest General Motors Recall, which is massive, and there may be lawsuits involved in the near future. And I predict that there will be. Who did they call? They called me this morning. There's going to be a story tomorrow on about that. So what that does is that boost my credibility level through the roof, which consumers say, okay, I really like those commercials and they're funny. But now we see that this guy's really serious about what he does and he's not just all fun and games, which that is important because of course, Nick, you know my brand and it's all about having fun, having a good time and having a different experience at our dealership than any other store in the world. We call it the Walt Disney or the Disney World of buying cars, but there's got to be that serious side because really, do you want to make a $30,000 investment on a used car from Mickey Mouse or from an amusement park? The answer is probably not. But when you put the two things together, something beautiful happens. You know, it creates a real person. It puts a real persona behind that image that you see on TV just in that 30 second commercial. So those of you who are watching at home, we did a documentary on Tracy called Carman about himself, the dealership, his family life. We really try to show his personal brand, not just the one side they see on TV. And what I love about Tracy is you did some awesome things with it from Red Carpet Premier all the way through to what you did on TV with it. So just give some of those secrets and then tell them what happened and we'll kind of undress some of those secrets. Sure. Well, you know, the Carman documentary was really, was really awesome and not only as a project, but what we did in our town of 500,000 people is we decided we were going to have a Red Carpet Premier because in our town, we don't have a professional sports team. We really don't have a lot of important big events that goes on. Celebrities don't come here often. So we actually put a Red Carpet downtown. We rented out a theater. We did a big Red Carpet Premier. We filled the theater. We actually filmed the folks going inside the theater. We got short interview snippets of them. And then what we did is we could use that footage as bonus footage on the DVD. We could use that as bonus footage on the website. And it also got those people involved in the whole Red Carpet Premier, which was awesome. And we put all that together. What actually happened was we had the news crew out there and the media. They were in the audience. They watched the film. And what they wanted to do is they wanted to actually show it on the local, I think it was the NBC affiliate that year, whoever was carrying the Super Bowl. And they ran it. And it was their highest rated programming of the day. So they actually picked the Carmen movie to be the lead in for the Super Bowl that year. And I keep in mind, the lead in for the Super Bowl in this market, I haven't priced it because one, I couldn't afford it and wouldn't buy it if I could afford it. But I would take a wild guess. 1500 bucks for a 30 second commercial is a lead in in the Super Bowl. So what tens of thousands of dollars for 30 minutes is lead into the Super Bowl. They actually played that for free. The ratings were through the roof. It beat all the other programming, which is fairly easy when you're the lead into the Super Bowl. But the whole point of that is not only did it tell my story, not only did it tell the story of my family, it was absolutely no cost to me at all. Now, of course, there was the investment of the movie and the time involved and all that. But it paid for itself in dividends and it's still paying for itself. I mean, people still come up to me and they say, man, I saw that thing where your dad had you in the in the detail shop, but I didn't know that. And that's so cool. And we play it consistently in this market. But the biggest thing I got for the butt was was that lead into the Super Bowl, which they picked up on. So it was awesome. Really smart. And now, even though, you know, you will that's gone, like most media, that was on and gone, right? And so but you get the credibility forever of having been the lead in the Super Bowl. Oh, absolutely. And not only that, you know, it was it was on the bio channel through the profiles, this is a success show. And we leveraged that as well. And about every 90 days or so, we'll bring that bring that back out. And on the anniversary dates, well, hey, it's the first anniversary of the six month anniversary. Or, you know, we'll do something. We're always trying to renew and regurgitate and renew that media, which, you know, every smart marketer does to some degree. Awesome. I love that. And so, you know, you've done a lot of stuff with us, just, you know, multiple bestselling books, we've done a movie, you've been on the Brian Tracy show, which we've done a lot of stuff. And what I love about, I tell people your example all the time. And I tell them, you know, what Tracy did was you have a physical location of business. I mean, some people have online businesses, service business, you're a physical brick and mortar, you know, kind of a corner store type business, if you will. And when people walk in and they see the stuff you've got on the walls behind you, you got all the media placements, you got the Amazon bestseller charts, you've got your books around, you've got the golden reel up from the movie, you've got the movie out. I mean, they're no longer walking into the office of a car dealer, a car salesman who wants to sell them a car. They're walking into the office of a celebrity that's willing to help them out with their problem. And I think that that's, if people could understand just that one shift of getting people to see you as more than a salesman, as seeing, and even more than a solution provider, you're literally this hometown celebrity that's willing to help them out with their issue. There's literally then no competition. You've created it so that there isn't any competition. Sure, other dealerships, people might check out and try to price shop or whatever. But if they want the Tracy Myers, the branded experience, if they want to ring the bell and be on the red carpet, get their photo taken afterwards, you've built the entire business around your personality. And one of the cool things I think is important to share with everybody too is that you're not always there. You're two people, the biggest objection I get with a lot of people with personal branding is that, well, I don't want to be there all the time. I can't take everyone's phone call. Look, you're gone 80, 90% of the time out speaking at events, teaching at events, doing cool stuff. So talk a little bit about how leveraging your personality through what I call one to many experiences, but they feel like one to one because of the way you're branding them, how that's helped grow your business as well. Sure. So, you know, it's really smart. And I really, I got that from an early age and it's like we talked about in the carman documentary. I'm not a car guy per se. I'm a car guy by default on a car dealership. But for me, it was always about the experience. I couldn't understand as a teenager, I couldn't understand how we could sell the same cars to do up the street and make more money. Or I guess the bigger question was, you know, why would they choose us over our competitor? You know, what made us all different? Because I would go into car dealerships with my father as he was buying cars from the other car dealerships. And I was like, man, this, this, I'm bored of tears. This place, this is not a place I would want to hang out. And it's funny because all the time customers say, I mean, this is really a cool thing you've done for your customers. And half jokingly and half not, I say, well, you know, I really didn't do this for the customers. I did this for me. I did this for the team because we're here 10, 12, 13 hours a day when we're actually here. So we want to have a good time. And we parlay that to the customers because we feel like if we're having a good time, obviously they're going to have a good time as well. So you're absolutely right. All the different opportunities that I have gotten from, and a lot of it did come from the credibility I gained from the books. And there's, I can't tell you the countless things, especially from Brian Tracy. And, but I'm gone 70, like you said, 70, 80% of the time I'm helping other dealers. I'm traveling, traveling the country. I'm in Canada. I've been real fortunate and that's what I enjoy doing now. Now, of course, I still, I've created the vision. I've got to share the vision and make sure the vision gets implemented with my team members. And that's my primary focus right now and keeping my ports on social media, because that's where my customers are, but I'm not here a whole lot. So it's really important for me to, when customers walk in, they say, Oh, that's the guy on TV, just like Jeremy, Jeremy from yesterday. He said, Oh, that's the guy from TV. That's the guy I came to see. They walk in and they see things like the Amazon bestseller awards or the golden reel. We've got Carmen playing in our law course. It's 22 minutes, it's 28 and a half minutes with, with the bonus footage. So customers here a couple of three hours and they get tired of watching that. So we've got it in, we had a closed caption version created and it's playing over in the corner while the radio TV's playing. So it does diminish the experience for people who are here longer, but they still say, I know that guy, I know that person. So it's almost not quite, but it's almost like I'm here because they know who I am. They see my image and they put two and two together and they say, Okay, this is where I'm supposed to be because just like with any good marketing piece, Nick, and you know this because you're an expert marketer and I've learned so much from you that, everything has to connect. So if, if, and I can't, you've done this as well, when you go into a store and you see a commercial on TV or radio or you get a direct mail piece, even sit on the internet and you go and say, Hey, I'm here for, for the X, the, the, the sale that I saw on the paper and the sales representative says, Oh, what X is that? What sale is that? It kills everything on contact. Now they can build that back up and recover if they're really good, but the reality of it is most of them aren't very good and they can never recover because subconsciously that's been crushed in the customer's mind. However, when they walk in here, I want to put it in default mode almost to where, Hey, I'm here. That's what the customer says subconsciously. They look around everything that they've, they, they have seen in the media, whether it's Carmen, whether it's me on Fox news or doing one of my expert segments on a news program, or they've seen one of my books or they follow us on social media. It all connects everything and it creates a synergy and in the customer's mind, subconsciously, once again, it's like, okay, everything's good here because everything connects, everything clicks. That makes sense. And what you're doing really, you're using that business trifecta. You're using media and PR in your market and you're using the credibility of that media and PR to drive the sales. One of the things that we teach Dan and I in the, the living legend formula is kind of the five phases of celebrity branding and the first three, you know, finding your niche, building your brand and then developing your celebrity expert status are, are three that you've done exceptionally well. What would you say to, to somebody who would think, you know, look this, I'm not a car dealer. I don't sell goods. I'm, maybe I'm an internet marketer. Talk a little bit about how, how you think this can work for anybody and the fact that it looked media credentials and PR when you understand these concepts and how they work together and that you can build your credibility through things like best selling books and blogs and articles and press. So just content in general. Talk about what a shift that you think that can make for anybody. Sure. So like most business owners or entrepreneurs or someone looking for a return investment, I'll give you some, one example. And I could literally give, realistically, I could give 20, 30, 40, 50, if I thought about it long enough and actually had a voice and wasn't afraid of losing the rest of it today. However, I'll give you one and I knew early on, I said, man, I'm going to do everything I possibly can with Nick and his great team over there because I think one of the first projects that we did was a Brian Tracy show. Like a lot of people, especially in sales, I thought Brian Tracy forever, you know, I used to give copies of, of Eat That Frog to every salesperson that came through the door. So it was really important in my life. I was on the Brian Tracy show and he actually coined a phrase. And you know how that show works, right? And it's like, it's like, boom, you sit down, he talks to you for a minute, minute and a half. Thanks. You feel real warm and fuzzy, which he can do like nobody else can. He knocks the interview out. You're thinking, man, this is really going to stink, but he walks you through the whole thing. He kills it. And, but in that minute and a half, I was explaining what we did and how we try to make things different. He said, oh, you're kind of like a Disney world for, for car buyers. I'm like, oh my god. You know, I couldn't explain it any better than you just did is what I'm thinking. He used that during the interview. And of course, I take that quote and I'm putting that on my personal branding, my consulting website, right? Well, I wrote a press release about what Brian Tracy said or what he, the phrase he coined for me and what he called me, the Walt Disney for a new generation or something like that. Uh, and somebody was doing a search for Walt Disney just, I mean, uh, for Brian Tracy. He just so happy. He pulled up that press release. He owned, uh, a card, uh, uh, a huge, um, uh, bunch of card dealerships, uh, New Card dealerships and he called me. He found me on Google because he was searching Brian Tracy found that press release. They mentioned Brian Tracy called me that resulted in real money, $50,000 account. So, uh, I don't think there needs to be any other examples. Uh, Realistically was because that wasn't brick and mortar. That was consulting. Yeah, a great example. And, and one things we teach also in, in the living legend formula is the power of press releases. Press releases when used correctly, we talk about there's kind of really two types of press releases. There's things, there's press releases you put out, uh, for the media to get and get a hold of before something big happens or after something big happens, but then there's also a ton of stuff you can do online with PR that makes people find you on Google. And it really changes the game for you. So that's an awesome example. And again, uh, one of the things we teach in the living legend formula, if you don't get anything else out of this video, understand the power of syndicating online press releases because they really do work. They can end up in, I mean, they can get you in all sorts of things. I mean, I get so much media like, uh, I've been in the USA today recently a couple times, um, quoted on Britney Spears' new show in Las Vegas of all things. And I was in business week not too long ago talking about, uh, Gwyneth Paltrow's business. And it's like, it's, it's crazy. And really they find me because of my blogs, my articles, my press releases that are online. And that's how I get media. You know, media begets more media. You get on major TV stations and major TV interviews because they've seen you on TV. They've seen your, uh, the carman documentary, they go to your website. They see your, you got blogs, you got, they know you've got content and they've seen you on video. One of these people don't realize is you can't catch fish if there's no worm in the water. So this is one of the ways you can put the worm in the water. So, uh, great example, Tracy. And hey man, I know you've got to run, but, uh, we're going to move on to our next interview. Let's talk a little bit about how once you started owning that personal brand of the ROI guy, kind of what changed for you? You know, what changes is credibility, recognition. I think recognition and credibility are the two largest components. So a lot of people say, well, did it increase your ROI? Well, the reality is, of course it did. Now, the second answer that is it gave me the chance to be in front of everybody first. I had to deliver on the back end, but I had, I was to market first. I was in their minds most recently. And when they needed something, they immediately thought of the ROI guy. So I had the first chance to solve their problem because of my branding and everything that was built around the ROI guy. I love it. Now, Dan Kennedy and I are going to be revealing pretty soon the actual living legend formula. And he really laid out the principles and the recipes that you can follow step by step to create your own legendary status. And for me, it was just like an aha moment that one I'll never forget. That's cool. I want to ask you this question now. Like, so from knowing what you know now, what would you go back and tell startup ROI guy 15, 20 years ago? What would you tell him? You know, I would have believed that I could have been a legend. I think early on I was trying to fill a void and I did not believe that I could be that guy on TV one day. I never believed I could be a bestselling author. So I would have the mindset number one that I could do it. I could be a legend. And then you work the recipe from there. I think believing in mindset is a crucial beginning that a lot of people don't have when they're just starting out and creating their own brand. That's great. Now, one of the things that I hate when people might be hearing this and they might be early in their career, they might say, man, I'm so far from living legends. People are going to remember you. They may not buy your service or your package right today. But the reality is they know what you do. It sticks. So you got that whole stick factor when they do need your service. They're going to remember you first.
